original_title: TeamPCP Pushes Malicious Telnyx Versions to PyPI, Hides Stealer in WAV Files
selection_reasoning: Supply chain attacks that hide malware in unexpected file formats showcase how attackers exploit trust in established trade routes and use creative concealment methods, offering rich lessons about hidden dangers in trusted systems.

field_note.title: Poison in the Well
field_note.subtitle: When trusted trade routes carry corruption disguised as song
field_note.excerpt: The most insidious corruptions travel not as obvious threats, but as gifts bearing familiar seals.
field_note.epigraph: Even the sweetest melody may carry venom in its notes.
body_paragraphs: The merchant guild known as TeamPCP has once again demonstrated the perverse creativity that marks the most dangerous of trade route poisoners. This time, they corrupted packages traveling the great Python trading network, hiding their harvest-spirits not in the usual scrolls or ledgers, but woven into the very fabric of musical recordings - those humble WAV melodies that few would suspect of harboring malice.

What elevates this attack beyond mere audacity is the sophistication of its concealment. By embedding their credential-stealing enchantments within audio files, the attackers understood that most protective wards scan for obvious threats - suspicious code, known corruption patterns, dangerous permissions. Few guardians think to examine a simple song file for hidden malevolence, making it an nearly perfect vessel for smuggling.

The choice of target reveals equally careful planning. The Telnyx trading house had established such trust within the Python merchant networks that few questioned packages bearing its seal. This trust, built over years of reliable service, became the very weapon used against those who depended on it. The attackers didn't need to build their own reputation - they simply stole one that already existed.

This incident joins a growing collection of corrupted trade route attacks that should concern every kingdom's supply masters. When even musical files can carry curses, and trusted merchant houses can be impersonated with ease, the old methods of verification prove inadequate. The age demands new forms of vigilance, ones that assume corruption may hide in any form, behind any familiar seal.
